ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised lamb tripe with radish. FOODON_03315595 (braised food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sheep offal) is used as the closest parent. No specific code exists for braised lamb tripe with radish and spicy-sour seasoning.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not provide granular codes for Chinese braised offal dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of spicy and sour radish braised with lamb tripe is approximately 300 g, intended as a shared dish for 2–3 diners alongside rice or other dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked lamb tripe and white radish composite — estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries: Lamb, variety meats and by-products, stomach, cooked, simmered (FDC ID 172546) and Radish, raw (FDC ID 169276), adjusted for braising with soy sauce, vinegar, chili, garlic, and sesame oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: lamb stomach (cooked, simmered) weighted at approximately 55% of dish mass, white radish (cooked) at approximately 35%, and seasoning/sauce components (soy sauce, vinegar, chili, sesame oil) at approximately 10%. Values are blended proportionally and adjusted for sodium contribution from soy sauce. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed offal and vegetable braise estimated from analogous low-GI components: cooked daikon radish (GI ~15, Atkinson et al. 2008) and braised meat dish analogue (GI ~30–35); composite GI estimated at 32
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GI is estimated at 32 (low) based on the dominant low-GI components: cooked white radish (daikon) GI approximately 15 per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283), and braised lamb/offal dishes which contribute negligible glycemic load due to high protein and fat content. The carbohydrate content is low (4.1 g per 100 g), yielding a glycemic load per 300 g serving of approximately 4.0 g, which is low. Composite GI of 32 is a conservative estimate consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database methodology for mixed dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,560 mg per 300 g serving), this dish contributes a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals and should be consumed sparingly or with reduced-sodium soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 4.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 32, this dish has a low glycemic load (approximately 4 g per serving) and is unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol from lamb tripe and moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb tripe contributes approximately 95 mg cholesterol and 1.6 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, and note that the high sodium content further elevates cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy and Sour Radish Braised with Lamb Tripe (酸辣萝卜烩羊肚, Suān là luóbo huì yáng dǔ) is a Chinese braised offal dish featuring slow-cooked lamb tripe (羊肚, yáng dǔ) and white radish (daikon) in a spicy, vinegar-forward broth seasoned with chili, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is characterised by a mildly sour and mildly spicy flavour profile (微酸，微辣) with a pleasantly chewy texture from the tripe and a crisp bite from the radish. It is served as a shared dish in Chinese restaurants in Singapore, including at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局). Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with 10.2 g protein and 4.8 g fat. The sodium content is high at approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to soy-based seasoning, which is a primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Lamb tripe is a good source of vitamin B12 (approximately 1.2 mcg per 100 g), zinc, and iron, supporting haematological health. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding soy or sesame allergens. It is not certified halal due to the use of lamb tripe from non-halal-certified sources in typical restaurant preparation.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this dish exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked lamb stomach and white radish, blended proportionally. GI is an analogous estimate based on component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (lamb stomach cooked simmered FDC ID 172546; radish raw FDC ID 169276) blended at approximately 55% lamb tripe, 35% radish, 10% seasoning. No direct dish entry exists in any primary approved source. 2. Sodium is estimated at 520 mg per 100 g, reflecting significant soy sauce contribution; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for lamb stomach or radish and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is marked false as lamb tripe sourced from non-halal-certified suppliers is common in Chinese restaurant contexts; this should be verified per restaurant. 7. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) in Singapore at SGD 32.50 per serving.
